reasoning: The finding claims reflected XSS via filter_input(INPUT_GET, $taxonomy) passed as 'selected' to wp_dropdown_categories(). After tracing the full data flow, the GET value is not reflected into HTML output. WordPress's Walker_CategoryDropdown::start_el() uses the 'selected' parameter exclusively in a PHP equality comparison (via the selected() helper function which calls __checked_selected_helper()). The attacker-controlled string is compared against each category's slug/term_id value to determine whether to emit the 'selected="selected"' HTML attribute. The option value attributes in the generated markup are populated from the database (esc_attr($category->{$r->value_field})), not from the GET input. There is no code path by which the raw GET string reaches any HTML sink. The hook (restrict_manage_posts) fires only in wp-admin and requires at minimum edit_posts capability, so the attack surface is limited to authenticated admins/editors regardless -- but the primary reason this is a false positive is the absence of any XSS sink: the user-supplied value never appears in the HTML response.
